description: The visible image is obscured by a 'Check for updates' user-interface element, rendering the scientific content unreadable. Based on the provided textual context, this figure is inferred to present engineering and true stress–strain curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels after 50 % cold rolling and subsequent annealing at temperatures ranging from 873 K to 1273 K. The curves likely compare tensile behavior across three alloys: 28Mn0Ni (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.9C), 28Mn5Ni (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni), and 15Mn5Ni (Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni). Contextual data indicate that 28Mn0Ni achieves up to ~86 % elongation when annealed at 1173 K, while Ni-containing alloys display varied strength–ductility balances, and the 15Mn5Ni alloy was too brittle at 973 K and 1023 K to record reliable curves. The figure would demonstrate how Mn/Ni ratio and annealing temperature govern the strength–ductility trade-off.

Figure 1 shows EBSD results for three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels—28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni—after hot rolling and 50% cold rolling (HR+CR). Each row presents colored inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for austenite ((a), (d), (g)), image quality (IQ) maps ((b), (e), (h)), and phase maps ((c), (f), (i)), with a 20 μm scale bar. The 28Mn0Ni alloy is single-phase FCC (red) with deformation slip bands marked by a blue arrow in (b). The 28Mn5Ni alloy is primarily austenite with minor B2 intermetallic (blue) particles in (f). The 15Mn5Ni alloy exhibits significant elongated B2 stringers (blue in (i)) within the FCC matrix; the inset IPF of B2 in (g) and yellow arrows indicate shearing and fragmentation of B2 during rolling. The figure establishes that lower Mn and Ni addition increase the B2 fraction and cause heterogeneous co-deformation, where hard B2 stringers fragment and align along the rolling direction, influencing subsequent recrystallization and precipitation.

Figure 2 presents X-ray diffraction patterns comparing phase evolution in two Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels: (a) Ni-free 28Mn0Ni and (b) Ni-containing 15Mn5Ni, following hot rolling plus cold rolling (HR+CR) and subsequent annealing at 973 K or 1173 K. In the as-cold-rolled state, both alloys exhibit only austenite γ (FCC) peaks. After annealing at 973 K, additional diffraction peaks emerge in both alloys, corresponding to κ-carbide (red circles) and B2 intermetallic (blue triangles), confirming concurrent precipitation. The B2 peaks are markedly more intense in 15Mn5Ni than in 28Mn0Ni, indicating that Ni addition promotes B2 formation, especially at lower Mn levels. After higher-temperature annealing at 1173 K, the 28Mn0Ni alloy reverts to a single γ-phase pattern, indicating dissolution of both κ-carbide and B2 precipitates. In contrast, 15Mn5Ni retains pronounced B2 peaks at 1173 K while κ-carbide peaks disappear, demonstrating higher thermal stability of B2 in the Ni-bearing, lower-Mn composition. These results establish that Mn/Ni ratio strongly dictates precipitation sequences and dissolution temperatures during post-deformation heat treatment.

Figure 3 presents EBSD-based colored IPF, phase contrast, and KAM maps for three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels—28Mn0Ni [(a)-(c)], 28Mn5Ni [(d)-(f)], and 15Mn5Ni [(g)-(i)]—after hot rolling, 50% cold rolling, and annealing at 1023 K. Subfigures (a, d, g) show austenite IPF maps with respect to the rolling direction (RD) and normal direction (ND), (b, e, h) display phase distributions with FCC in red and B2 in blue, and (c, f, i) give KAM maps scaled from 0 to 5. The Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy exhibits nearly fully recrystallized equiaxed austenite grains with low KAM values and only sparse B2 particles. The 28Mn5Ni alloy shows a partially recrystallized microstructure with elongated deformed grains, scattered B2, and heterogeneous KAM; the inset of (d) caption states fine recrystallized austenite grains at higher magnification. The 15Mn5Ni alloy displays a banded two-phase microstructure containing a substantial volume fraction of B2 stringers and high KAM values indicating retained deformation. These maps collectively demonstrate that increasing Ni and decreasing Mn content suppress recrystallization and promote B2 intermetallic formation during annealing at 1023 K.

Figure 4 displays colored EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for the austenite phase and corresponding phase maps in Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based low-density steels subjected to hot rolling plus cold rolling followed by annealing at 973 K or 1173 K. Subfigures (a) and (b) present 28Mn0Ni, (c) and (d) present 28Mn5Ni, and (e) and (f) present 15Mn5Ni, with the left column for 973 K and the right column for 1173 K. The B2-free 28Mn0Ni alloy shows partial recrystallization at 973 K and pronounced grain coarsening at 1173 K, while the Ni-containing alloys retain deformed austenite grains and secondary phases at both temperatures. Phase maps (g) and (h) for 15Mn5Ni reveal that the B2 phase (blue) is abundant at 973 K but dissolves substantially at 1173 K, leaving FCC austenite (red) dominant. The inset in (f) provides the IPF map for B2 alone, illustrating recovered bamboo-type grains. These observations demonstrate that the presence of B2 particles hinders austenite recrystallization and grain growth during two-phase region heat treatment.

This figure presents four quantitative subplots derived from OIM-EBSD analysis showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni) after 50% cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment at temperatures ranging from 973 K to 1173 K. Panel (a) tracks the recrystallized austenite fraction, panel (b) the mean grain size of recrystallized austenite, panel (c) the twin fraction, and panel (d) the secondary B2 phase volume fraction as functions of heat treatment temperature. The 28Mn0Ni alloy exhibits the highest recrystallization kinetics, reaching full recrystallization near 1173 K with significant grain growth to approximately 40 μm and the highest twin fraction (~0.6). In contrast, the Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) show markedly delayed recrystallization, finer recrystallized grains (<6 μm even at 1173 K), and lower twin fractions, with 15Mn5Ni being the most sluggish. The B2 phase fraction decreases with increasing temperature for 15Mn5Ni (from ~0.4 to ~0.15) and largely dissolves in 28Mn0Ni, while horizontal dashed lines in (d) denote the prior hot-rolled B2 volume fractions. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that higher Mn promotes recrystallization and annealing twinning, whereas Ni and stable B2 particles retard recrystallization and constrain grain growth.

TEM bright-field (a, c, e, g) and dark-field (b, d, f, h) micrographs together with selected-area electron diffraction insets reveal the precipitation behavior in 28Mn0Ni (a–d), 28Mn5Ni (e,f), and 15Mn5Ni (g,h) Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels after hot rolling, cold rolling, and annealing at 973 K. Bright-field images show the austenite matrix and intergranular B2 particles (solid white arrows), while dark-field images captured from the (010)κ satellite spot highlight κ-carbide distributions (dotted orange arrows). Recrystallized austenite regions contain fine uniform intragranular κ-carbides, whereas deformed regions contain inhomogeneous coarser κ-carbides of 10–60 nm. The 15Mn5Ni alloy exhibits larger κ-carbides than 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni under identical conditions. Additionally, Ni-containing alloys display fine intragranular B2 precipitates (5–20 nm, broken yellow arrows) and intergranular B2 (50–200 nm). These observations demonstrate that κ-carbide and B2 precipitation is strongly influenced by local deformation state and Ni content, with deformed austenite favoring coarser carbides and Ni addition enhancing B2 formation.

Figure 7 presents TEM bright-field images comparing the microstructures of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels—28Mn0Ni [(a,b)], 28Mn5Ni [(c,d)], and 15Mn5Ni [(e,f)]—after 50% cold rolling and heat treatment at 1173 K. For the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy, images (a) and (b) show fully recrystallized austenite (γ) grains with no visible secondary-phase precipitates. In contrast, the Ni-containing alloys display B2 intermetallic particles, marked by white and yellow arrows, existing as both intragranular and intergranular phases within partially recrystallized austenite matrices. The micrographs reveal a systematic decrease in intragranular B2 precipitation with increasing recrystallized austenite fraction, indicating that highly deformed, high-dislocation-density regions act as preferential nucleation sites for B2. Combined with TEM-EDS data reported in the text, these images support the conclusion that Ni additions stabilize B2 phase formation, while elevated heat-treatment temperature (1173 K) leads to complete precipitate dissolution in the Ni-free alloy but only partial reduction of B2 in Ni-bearing steels.

Figure 8 comprises two scatter plots showing Vickers hardness (VHN) variations in three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels: 28Mn0Ni (Ni-free), 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni. Subfigure (a) plots hardness versus cold rolling reduction (0–50% reduction in thickness). All alloys exhibit increasing hardness with deformation; 15Mn5Ni starts highest (~380 VHN at 0%) but work-hardens such that all three converge to ~430–460 VHN at 50% reduction. Subfigure (b) plots hardness versus post-cold-rolling heat treatment temperature (873–1273 K, 1 h WQ). Hardness generally decreases with increasing annealing temperature. The Ni-free 28Mn0Ni shows the steepest drop (from ~430 VHN at 873 K to ~190 VHN at 1273 K), while 15Mn5Ni maintains the highest hardness across the entire window (~500 VHN at 873 K to ~300 VHN at 1273 K), attributed to higher volume fractions of B2 intermetallics and κ-carbides that retard softening. 28Mn5Ni shows intermediate behavior. Collectively, the data reveal that Ni and Mn content strongly govern work-hardening response and thermal stability of hardness, with higher alloy contents promoting precipitation-strengthening that sustains elevated hardness despite high-temperature annealing.

Figure 9 assembles tensile deformation data for three austenitic low-density steels—28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni—in HR+CR and heat-treated conditions. Subfigure (a) provides engineering stress-engineering strain curves in the as-cold-rolled state, revealing that 15Mn5Ni attains the highest peak stress (~1800 MPa) but fractures earliest, whereas 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni display comparable strengths (~1500–1600 MPa) with somewhat better elongation. Subfigure (b) shows engineering curves after annealing at 973 K, 1023 K, and 1173 K, where 28Mn0Ni exhibits marked softening and exceptional ductility at 1173 K, while the Ni-containing alloys retain higher flow stresses and moderate ductility. Subfigure (c) presents corresponding true stress-true strain curves for selected conditions, and (d) gives ln(σ) versus ln(ε) plots from which strain-hardening exponents n are derived. All curves display two-slope characteristics with small n at low strain and substantially higher n at large strain. The derived n values increase from 0.16 (973 K) to 0.50 (1173 K) for 28Mn0Ni, while both Ni-bearing alloys stabilize near ~0.30 at 1173 K. These results support the conclusion that the Ni-free alloy possesses superior strain-hardening capacity, linked to its distinct recrystallization behavior and precipitation states of κ-carbides and B2 particles compared with the Ni-containing compositions.

Figure 10 presents fractographic and cross-sectional microstructural analysis of failed tensile specimens from Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels after hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and heat treatment at 1023 K. Panels (a–c) show SEM fractographs of (a) 28Mn0Ni, (b) 28Mn5Ni, and (c) 15Mn5Ni alloys. Both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni exhibit dimple morphology characteristic of ductile fracture, whereas 15Mn5Ni displays a distinctive quasi-cleavage surface with numerous microcracks, indicating crack-initiation-controlled brittle failure. Panels (d–f) examine the cross-section of the 15Mn5Ni failed specimen: (d) an SEM image revealing intergranular cracks, (e) an OIM-IPF orientation map, and (f) a phase contrast map showing FCC (red) and B2 (blue) distributions. The EBSD data demonstrate that crack propagation follows γ/B2 interphase boundaries. Together, these results reveal that reduced Mn and added Ni in 15Mn5Ni suppress austenite recrystallization and promote extensive B2 precipitation along grain boundaries, causing intergranular and interphase brittle fracture that explains its markedly inferior tensile ductility relative to the higher-Mn alloys.

This figure presents a set of TEM bright-field images capturing the post-tensile deformed microstructures of the Ni-free Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel designated 28Mn0Ni. Panel (a) shows the microstructure of the HR+CR+973 K sample deformed to a true strain of ε~0.14, revealing dislocation multiplication and cell structure formation within partially recrystallized austenite. Panels (b–e) display the HR+CR+1173 K fully recrystallized condition at increasing tensile strains: (b) at ε~0.02 shows initial planar slip bands; (c) at ε~0.08 exhibits intersecting slip bands; (d) at ε~0.30 demonstrates progressive refinement of slip band spacing; and (e) highlights interactions between dislocations and prior annealing twins. The sequence from (b) to (d) illustrates dynamic slip band refinement with applied strain, while (e) evidences dislocation-twin interaction as a key deformation mechanism. Collectively, these images support the conclusion that the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy develops homogeneous slip band spacing during deformation and that planar slip activity, slip band refinement, and dislocation-twin interactions govern the strain hardening behavior in fully recrystallized austenite.

This figure presents four TEM bright-field micrographs (a–d) revealing the post-tensile-deformation microstructures of the 15Mn5Ni alloy (Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni) processed under HR+CR+1173 K conditions (50 % cold rolled and annealed at 1173 K for 1 h), captured at increasing tensile strains of ε~0.02, ~0.05, and ~0.14. Panel (a) at ε~0.02 illustrates dislocation–B2 particle interactions, where curved dislocation lines are visible around coarse B2 precipitates. As strain increases to ε~0.05 in panel (b), extensive dislocation multiplication and tangling occur throughout the austenite matrix. Panels (c) and (d) at ε~0.14 show pronounced refinement and densification of planar slip bands with very high dislocation density. All micrographs carry a 200 nm scale bar. These images support the core conclusion that deformation in this partially recrystallized austenitic microstructure proceeds via planar slip, wherein B2 particles act as dislocation obstacles at low strain and dynamic slip band refinement provides sustained strain hardening at larger strains.
